key_insight: Discovery-relevant benchmarks show that many enzyme specificity models perform near random or no better than sequence baselines, while interaction-aware complex representations improve ranking.
engineering_relevance: For enzyme annotation, pathway design, and biocatalyst search, models must prioritize the true enzyme or reaction among many close candidates, not just score well on easier splits.
method: Benchmarked four recent ES/ER prediction models on tasks tailored to unseen enzymes and substrates.
Built a large CYP dataset with 2,922 reactions across 768 enzymes and a native-organism ranking benchmark.
Adapted Boltz by training supervised classifiers on residue-ligand pair embeddings for ES prediction.
evidence_signal: The study reports near-random performance for representative ES models on unseen-family settings, and BLAST remained competitive or superior to most models even after fine-tuning on the CYP ranking task.
watch_item: Performance gains may depend strongly on benchmark design and complex-embedding access, so deployment should avoid assuming that high scores on standard splits translate to real discovery settings.
paper_title: Rethinking Benchmarks and Models for Enzyme Specificity Prediction
